# Compliant Control {.section-divider}

## Why Position-Only Policies Are Not Enough

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Position commands alone do not specify interaction behavior.
- The same motion can be safe or unsafe depending on stiffness.
- Contact-rich manipulation needs motion and mechanical response.
:::

![](assets/figures/position_only_limitations.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: position commands alone do not define how the robot behaves when the environment resists.

Intuition: the same target pose can be gentle or dangerous depending on stiffness and force response.

Warning: do not frame this as "position control is bad"; it is insufficient by itself for many contact-rich tasks.
:::

---

## Position, Force, and Compliant Control

- Position control: track desired motion.
- Force control: regulate interaction force.
- Compliant control: shape the dynamic relation between force and motion.

::: {.notes}
Main point: distinguish the control objectives before introducing impedance and admittance.

Intuition: position asks "where should I be?", force asks "how hard should I push?", compliance asks "how should motion and force relate?".

Warning: real robot controllers often mix these categories, so treat them as conceptual anchors rather than strict boxes.
:::

---

## Impedance Control Intuition

Impedance control defines how the robot should generate forces in response to motion errors.

Useful mental model:

- high stiffness: precise but less forgiving
- low stiffness: safer but less accurate
- damping: dissipates energy and smooths response

::: {.notes}
Main point: impedance control shapes the apparent mechanical behavior of the robot.

Intuition: use the spring-damper analogy: stiffness is how strongly the robot resists displacement, damping is how it resists velocity.

Warning: high stiffness is not automatically better for precision if contact uncertainty creates large forces.
:::

---

## Admittance Control Intuition

Admittance control maps measured external force to a motion correction.

This is useful when the low-level robot interface accepts position or velocity commands.

::: {.notes}
Main point: admittance control converts measured forces into motion commands.

Intuition: if the environment pushes on the robot, the admittance law decides how much the reference should move in response.

Warning: students often invert impedance and admittance; impedance maps motion to force, admittance maps force to motion.
:::

---

## Mass-Spring-Damper Equation

A common admittance model is:

$$
M_d \Delta\ddot{x}_r + D_d \Delta\dot{x}_r + K_d \Delta x_r = F_{ext}
$$

where $M_d$, $D_d$, and $K_d$ are desired mass, damping, and stiffness, and $\Delta x_r$ is the force-induced reference displacement.

::: {.notes}
Main point: present the admittance equation as a desired dynamic relation, not a literal physical mass-spring-damper attached to the robot.

Intuition: the controller simulates how a virtual mass, damper, and spring would move under the measured external force.

Warning: choosing gains poorly can produce unstable or sluggish interaction, especially with delays and noisy force sensing.
:::

---

## Variable Stiffness and Damping

Instead of fixed gains, the controller can adapt:

$$
K_d = K_d(t, o_t),
\qquad
D_d = D_d(t, o_t)
$$

This creates task-dependent compliance.

::: {.notes}
Main point: variable gains allow the robot to be stiff or compliant depending on task phase and observations.

Intuition: during insertion, the robot may approach stiffly in free space and become more compliant near contact.

Warning: changing gains online is powerful but risky; abrupt or unconstrained gain changes can destabilize behavior.
:::

---

## Stability and Safety Intuition

- Learned parameters must remain within safe bounds.
- Fast changes in stiffness or damping can destabilize interaction.
- Passivity and energy-based reasoning provide useful guardrails.

![](assets/figures/compliance_safety_bounds.svg){width=70%}

::: {.notes}
Main point: learned control parameters must be constrained by safety and stability reasoning.

Intuition: a neural network can propose gains, but a safety filter should enforce allowed ranges and rate limits.

Warning: empirical success is not the same as stability; contact tasks need conservative deployment assumptions.
:::
